description: Primary-sourced data-visualization rules — encoding accuracy, axis integrity, chart choice, color, and chartjunk. Re-derived from Cleveland & McGill's encoding ranking, Tufte, and the Datawrapper/colorbrewer accessibility work.

rules.title: Encode by perceptual accuracy
rules.rule: Position on a common scale is judged most accurately, then length, then angle/slope, then area, then color/volume (Cleveland & McGill). Prefer bar/line (position+length) over pie (angle) and bubble (area) for precise comparison.
rules.dont: A pie chart for >3 categories or close values.

rules.title: Bar charts start the value axis at zero
rules.rule: Bars encode value by LENGTH, so a truncated baseline lies about ratios — start bar axes at 0. Line charts (encoding position/trend) may use a non-zero, clearly-labeled range.
rules.dont: A bar chart with a y-axis starting at 90 exaggerating tiny diffs.

rules.title: Match chart type to the question
rules.rule: Comparison→bar; trend over time→line; part-to-whole→stacked bar/treemap (rarely pie); correlation→scatter; distribution→histogram/box. Pick by the relationship, not aesthetics.

rules.title: Maximize data-ink, remove chartjunk
rules.rule: Erase non-data ink — heavy gridlines, 3D effects, gradients, redundant legends. Every mark should carry information (Tufte's data-ink ratio).

source.name: Tufte — The Visual Display of Quantitative Information

rules.title: Accessible, meaningful color scales
rules.rule: Use colorblind-safe palettes (ColorBrewer/viridis); choose sequential for ordered data, diverging for a midpoint, categorical (<= ~7 hues) for nominal. Never rely on color alone — add labels/patterns. Maintain contrast.

rules.title: Label directly; minimize lookups
rules.rule: Place labels next to the data (line ends, bar tips) instead of forcing eyes to a separate legend; reduce the cognitive round-trip. Annotate the insight.
